title: Spritz biscuits
subtitle: Piped butter biscuits in the German and Scandinavian tradition
excerpt: A classic piped butter biscuit from northern European baking: a very soft, pliable dough pressed through a star nozzle into rosettes, rings, or S-shapes. Crisp, buttery, and barely sweet.
sourceNotes: Spritz (or spritzgebäck) biscuits are a staple of German and Scandinavian Christmas baking, well documented in nineteenth-century German household cookery books. The name derives from the German 'spritzen' meaning to squirt or pipe. Similar butter biscuits appear across Danish, Swedish, and Dutch baking traditions. The recipe here follows the classic approach: a soft, pliable dough piped through a star nozzle onto ungreased baking trays.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked in an airtight container between sheets of baking paper.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The baked biscuits keep well in a tin for up to 2 weeks.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dough must be soft enough to pipe smoothly but stiff enough to hold a defined shape after piping. It should move through a star nozzle without excessive force but hold its ridges on the tray. If the dough tears when piped, it is too cold; if it spreads and loses definition, it is too warm.

content.text: The success of spritz biscuits depends on dough at exactly the right consistency: what bakers call

. The dough should pipe without tearing and hold its ridges without spreading. Beat the butter thoroughly until pale and fluffy rather than just softened, so the dough is smooth enough to pass through a nozzle cleanly.

Beat the butter and icing sugar together for at least 5 minutes until very pale and fluffy. Add the egg yolks and vanilla and beat until smooth. Sift in the flour and salt and mix until just combined. The dough should be soft, smooth, and pipeable. Use immediately; do not refrigerate before piping or it will be too stiff.

Preheat the oven to 175°C conventional. Do not grease or line the trays — the biscuits grip the bare tray surface as they are piped, which helps them hold their shape. Fill a piping bag fitted with a large open star nozzle. Pipe rosettes (hold the bag upright, squeeze, pull up), rings (pipe a circle and join the ends), or S-shapes directly onto the trays, spacing them 3 cm apart. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until pale gold at the edges. The tops should be barely coloured. Cool on the trays for 5 minutes; they are fragile when hot. Transfer to a rack.
